description:
You are a technical writer who maintains a small documentation site.  
    Inside the directory  /home/user/docs  there is already a CSV file named  index.csv  that lists every article which must appear in the site’s navigation menu.  
    Your objective is to produce a JSON counterpart of that file so that the web-team can consume it directly from JavaScript.

    1. Don’t move, rename, or delete  /home/user/docs/index.csv.  
    2. Create a new file **/home/user/docs/index.json** that contains an *array* of objects, one object for every row in the CSV file, **in the same order** as they appear in the CSV.  
    3. Each object must have the following keys in the exact order shown below:  
       • "id" (number, no quotes around the numeric value)  
       • "title" (string)  
       • "section" (string)  
    4. The automated checker will ignore whitespace, but the textual contents must be exactly as shown below (case, spelling, and punctuation all matter):

       [
         {
           "id": 1,
           "title": "Introduction",
           "section": "Getting Started"
         },
         {
           "id": 2,
           "title": "Installation",
           "section": "Getting Started"
         },
         {
           "id": 3,
           "title": "Configuration",
           "section": "Advanced Topics"
         },
         {
           "id": 4,
           "title": "FAQ",
           "section": "Appendix"
         }
       ]

       (A trailing newline at the end of the file is optional.)

    5. Write the file entirely from the shell with a **single command** (a one-liner).  
       • You may use any standard POSIX utilities already available in a minimal Linux distribution (e.g., awk, sed, tr, paste, cut), or purpose-built tools such as jq or csvkit if they happen to be installed.  
       • Whatever tool you choose, the final observable effect must be the creation of  /home/user/docs/index.json  containing the exact logical data shown above.  
       • Do not create any temporary files outside  /home/user/docs. (Tools that create in-place temp files are fine; just make sure they are cleaned up automatically.)

    Goal recap: after you run your single command,  /home/user/docs/index.json  should exist and contain the JSON array shown above, representing the four rows of the original CSV.

    You do not need to display the JSON on screen unless you want to check your work.  The automated tests will inspect the file directly.
truth:
Initial filesystem state BEFORE the agent starts:

    1. Directory /home/user/docs  exists and is writable by the current (non-root) user.
    2. File /home/user/docs/index.csv  exists with the following exact contents (with a single LF at the end of each line):

       id,title,section
       1,Introduction,Getting Started
       2,Installation,Getting Started
       3,Configuration,Advanced Topics
       4,FAQ,Appendix

    Expected filesystem state AFTER successful completion:

    1. /home/user/docs/index.csv  remains unchanged.
    2. /home/user/docs/index.json  exists and contains the **minified** JSON string below
       (the test script will ignore insignificant whitespace, so pretty-printed or
       minified are both acceptable; the string shown here is what the grader will parse):

       [{"id":1,"title":"Introduction","section":"Getting Started"},{"id":2,"title":"Installation","section":"Getting Started"},{"id":3,"title":"Configuration","section":"Advanced Topics"},{"id":4,"title":"FAQ","section":"Appendix"}]

       A single trailing newline is permissible but not required.
    3. No other new files or directories should be present in  /home/user/docs.
name: task_000000_98e6d7ff
